dealKiller: Success-fee economics are the load-bearing leg (€400-600K of Year-2 cash) and they collide with three independent reality checks: (1) Horizon Europe success rates collapsed from 16% (2024) to ~9-12% (2025) on an 80% surge in submissions — the spec's 20-25% win-rate assumption (2-3 wins per 8-12 submits) is 2x the realistic baseline of 9-15% for industrial mid-cap proposals; (2) success-fee contingency arrangements paid to grant writers are NOT a recognized eligible subcontracting cost under the Horizon Europe Model Grant Agreement — beneficiaries can pay them privately but cannot charge them to the grant, and the EC's 2025 AI-disclosure tightening + best-value-for-money subcontracting rules make percentage-of-award fee structures reputationally fragile with corporate procurement and audit committees at exactly the listed buyers (Mytilineos, Titan, Helleniq); (3) PNO Innovation already ships AI-assisted proposal writing in 2025-26 and openly claims 3-4x EU-average win rates with 40-year domain depth — the structural cost advantage Dimitri assumes (€50-70K vs PNO's €120-250K) is real only if PNO doesn't compress price, and PNO has every incentive to defend a 600-person book before letting a Greek family-network shop pick off six anchor accounts.
patternMatches: Binary-outcome revenue with 12-18mo working capital lag masquerading as recurring services
Win-rate assumption 2x prevailing baseline (20-25% spec vs 9-15% Horizon reality)
Buyer-side procurement/audit-committee allergy to %-of-award fees in regulated EU programmes
Incumbent (PNO) already shipping the AI angle — moat collapses to relationship-only
Founder explicitly flagged distaste for the work that actually consumes month-9 hours (consortium herding) — the same trap mentioned in the spec's own kill condition
AI-disclosure regime (HE Standard Application Form p.32, 2025) means '85-90% AI' must be declared, eroding the very cost advantage that justifies the price
unitEconomicsConcern: Fixed-fee book at €40-80K x 8-12 tenders = €450-800K covers opex but offers no operating leverage — that is a senior-services book, not an AI-leveraged services firm. The €400-600K success-fee tail is (a) deferred 12-18mo post-award, (b) net of a realistic 10-12% win rate produces ~1 win/year not 2-3, so expected value is €150-250K not €400-600K, and (c) collectability is fragile when corporate clients realize the fee structure is a regulatory gray zone they'd rather not explain to their audit committee. Year-2 cash is more plausibly €600-900K than €800K-1.4M, and Year-3 doesn't compound unless the win rate proves out across a sample size (8-12/yr) that takes 3 years to even measure.
loadBearingAssumption: That six named Greek industrial mid-caps will simultaneously (a) terminate or sideline incumbent grant relationships with PNO/EY/Deloitte, (b) accept a hybrid fixed + success-fee structure their procurement teams have likely already flagged as non-standard for EU programmes, and (c) deliver Dimitri 8-12 high-stakes 80-200-page tenders/year — when each of those companies typically runs 1-3 IF/Horizon proposals annually and won't concentrate that risk with a new boutique until 1-2 wins are demonstrated. The phase-0 hypothesis (1 paid tender by Wk 16) is achievable; the steady-state hypothesis (8-12 tenders/yr from 6 accounts) requires near-monopoly share of those accounts' grant work, which is implausible in year 1.
alternativeThesis: Reframe as 'EU funding strategy + lead-author' advisory, NOT contingent grant-writing. Pure fixed retainer: €80-120K/account/year covering eligibility screening, winning-shape memos, narrative drafting, and consortium-partner sourcing for 1-2 priority tenders/yr — no success fee. Caps Year-2 cash at €500-700K (lower upside than spec) but (a) eliminates the Article-style regulatory fragility and procurement friction, (b) survives a 9% win-rate environment because revenue isn't outcome-linked, (c) aligns with Dimitri's stated preference for strategic narrative over consortium ops, (d) is structurally harder for PNO to undercut because PNO's value-add is hours-on-paper, not strategic positioning. If success fees stay, cap at 0.25% and structure as deferred bonus on collected milestone payments (not grant-award), invoiced to the client entity not the grant — sidesteps the eligibility question entirely. Phase-0 kill condition should add: if procurement at any of the named 6 flags the success-fee clause as non-standard, drop it and re-price the fixed retainer up by 30%.
